coredata.dc:title: Enhanced electrochemiluminescence bioassay triggered by intracellular leakage for detection of Escherichia coli
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Yuxin
Jiang, Shumin
Hu, Yachen
Gong, Jingming
coredata.dc:description: An intracellular leakage-trigged signal-on solid-state electrochemiluminescent (ECL) assay is developed for the detection of Escherichia coli (E. coli). A self-assembled multilayer ensemble of N, S co-doped carbon dots -poly dimethyl diallylammonium chloride grafted carbon nanospheres is used as ECL luminophores with peroxydisulfate (PS) ions as coreactants. The incorporation of molecularly imprinted electrospun nanofibers with the multilayer ensemble enables a robust, highly selective solid-state ECL probe without using any expensive and fragile biological receptor. Upon the imprinted E. coli exposed to the assay, under bactericidal effects of PS ions by destroying the integrity of E. coli cell membrane, intracellular leakage K+-triggered ECL enhancement is first disclosed via prompting the involved 1O2-mediated ECL process. Benefiting from the ECL enhancement upon increasing the concentration of E. coli, a unique intracellular leakage-trigged signal-on ECL system is created for sensing E. coli. Such a assay is proved to be highly specific and sensitive for sensing E. coli in the concentration range from 5 to 107 cfu mL−1, achieving a detection limit of 1 cfu mL−1 (S/N = 3). This label-free, simple and facile assay provides a promising point-of-care diagnostic tool for pathogen detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Intracellular leakage
Electrochemiluminescent
Escherichia coli
Molecularly imprinted electrospun nanofibers

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of the ECL sensor and ECL detection of E. coli
Characterization of ESNFs and the modified electrode
ECL behaviors of the bioassay and possible mechanism
Analytical performance for detection of E. coli

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Program of Introducing Talents of Discipline to Universities of China
Project for Application Foundation Frontier for Wuhan

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by National Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 22076057 , 21777052 ), the Project for Application Foundation Frontier for Wuhan (Grant 2019020701011486 ), and The Program of Introducing Talents of Discipline to Universities of China (111 program, B17019 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of the CNs (A) and ensemble of (dCDs/PDDA)3@CNs (B), Inset: the corresponding TEM images; (C) the Zeta potential of CNs dispersion, PDDA, dCDs, PDDA@CNs and (dCDs/PDDA)3@CNs ensemble.

SEM images of (A) Polystyrene alone electrospun fiber, (B) Polystyrene electrospun fiber with E. coli; (C) The fluorescence of unwashed ESNFs (Inset: the fluorescence of ESNFs after removing the imprinted E. coli); (D) UV–vis absorption spectra of E. coli dispersion before (a) and after (b) NINFs-(dCDs/PDDA)3@CNs/GCE and (c) ESNFs-(dCDs/PDDA)3@CNs/GCE immersed.

(A) EIS plot of (a) bare GCE, ESNFs-(dCDs/PDDA)3@CNs/GCE with E. coli (b) imprinted, (c) eluted and (d) rebounded in 0.1 M KCl containing 0.01 M [Fe(CN)6]3-/4-. The applied frequency ranging from 0.01 Hz to 100 kHz; (B) ECL-potential curves of (a) bare GCE, (b) CNs/GCE, (c) ESNFs/GCE, (d) (dCDs/PDDA)3@CNs/GCE, ESNFs-(dCDs/PDDA)3@CNs/GCE (e) before and (f) after immersing E. coli solution (103 cfu mL−1) (Inset: the ECL responses of ESNFs-(dCDs/PDDA)3@CNs/GCE with the addition of K+ alone from a to c, 0, 0.1 and 10.0 mM); (C) the XPS survey spectrum of the intracellular leakage (Inset: the high resolution XPS spectrum of K2p); (D) Effects of dissolved oxygen on ECL intensity of ESNFs-(dCDs/PDDA)3@CNs/GCE in (a) air-saturated, (b) N2-saturated, (c) N2-saturated solution containing k+ conditions; Effects of the related radical scavengers on ECL responses of ESNFs-(dCDs/PDDA)3@CNs/GCE containing (d) isopropanol, (e) furfuryl alcohol, (f) methanol, (g) thymol in the air-saturated PBS solution. Inset: ECL-potential curves of bare GCE in the (a) absence and (b) presence of k+.

Effects of (A) solution pH, (B) scan rate, (C) the concentration of PS, (D) the volume of the mixture, (E) the volume ratio of dispersion of (dCDs/PDDA)3@CNs versus ESNFs and (F) immersion time of the as-prepared assay in E. coli (103 cfu mL−1) solution on ECL intensity of ESNFs-(dCDs/PDDA)3@CNs/GCE.

(A) Effect of the concentration of E. coli on the ECL intensity of ESNFs-(dCDs/PDDA)3@CNs/GCE (from a to i, 0, 5, 10, 102, 103, 104, 105, 106 and 107 cfu mL−1); (B) the calibration curve of the ECL intensity against the concentration of E. coli; (C) ECL intensity of ESNFs-(dCDs/PDDA)3@CNs/GCE in the presence of each amino acid compound (100 μM) and bacterial with similarity of E. coli (104 cfu mL−1) without (red) and with (black) 103 cfu mL−1 E. coli, (D) Replicated ECL responses of ESNFs-(dCDs/PDDA)3@CNs/GCE toward E. coli (103 cfu mL−1). Supporting electrolyte: air-saturated 0.1 M pH 7.0 PBS containing 150 mM PS. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Schematic illustration of the principle of the intracellular leakage-trigged signal-on solid-state ECL assay for E. coli detection.

head.ce:title: Enhanced electrochemiluminescence bioassay triggered by intracellular leakage for detection of Escherichia coli

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Pesticide & Chemical Biology of Ministry of Education, International Joint Research Center for Intelligent Biosensing Technology and Health, College of Chemistry, Central China Normal University, Wuhan, 430079, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Pesticide & Chemical Biology of Ministry of Education
International Joint Research Center for Intelligent Biosensing Technology and Health

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Pesticide & Chemical Biology of Ministry of Education, International Joint Research Center for Intelligent Biosensing Technology and Health, College of Chemistry, Central China Normal University, Wuhan, 430079, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An intracellular leakage-trigged signal-on solid-state electrochemiluminescent (ECL) assay is developed for the detection of Escherichia coli (E. coli). A self-assembled multilayer ensemble of N, S co-doped carbon dots -poly dimethyl diallylammonium chloride grafted carbon nanospheres is used as ECL luminophores with peroxydisulfate (PS) ions as coreactants. The incorporation of molecularly imprinted electrospun nanofibers with the multilayer ensemble enables a robust, highly selective solid-state ECL probe without using any expensive and fragile biological receptor. Upon the imprinted E. coli exposed to the assay, under bactericidal effects of PS ions by destroying the integrity of E. coli cell membrane, intracellular leakage K+-triggered ECL enhancement is first disclosed via prompting the involved 1O2-mediated ECL process. Benefiting from the ECL enhancement upon increasing the concentration of E. coli, a unique intracellular leakage-trigged signal-on ECL system is created for sensing E. coli. Such a assay is proved to be highly specific and sensitive for sensing E. coli in the concentration range from 5 to 107 cfu mL−1, achieving a detection limit of 1 cfu mL−1 (S/N = 3). This label-free, simple and facile assay provides a promising point-of-care diagnostic tool for pathogen detection.

ce:section.ce:para: Bacterial infectious diseases have recently emerged as a serious global public health concern, and aroused substantial financial burdens. Escherichia coli (E. coli) is one of the most harmful pathogenic bacteria, causing serious illnesses and deaths through contaminated water and food (Li et al., 2020; Yan et al., 2018). Thus, to ensure safety and carry out accurate disease diagnosis, it is highly desirable to achieve sensitive and selective detection of E. coli. Traditional bacterial-detection methods, e.g. culture techniques and polymerase chain reaction are often time-consuming, thereby making timely prevention of epidemic outbreak difficult (Hao et al., 2019a). Tremendous efforts have been devoted to improve global health management through coupling specific recognition elements with various transducers, including colorimetric, electrochemical, fluorescent, quartz crystal microbalance resonators and electrochemiluminescent (ECL) bioassays (Amin et al., 2020; Asif et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2019; Russo et al., 2018; Sun et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2017). The above bioassays may be initiated by the induced steric-hindrance or activating the labeled signal-generating elements. Despite the tremendous achievements obtained in E. coli analysis, some of them still suffer from the costly biological receptors and lengthy analysis procedures. In addition, during running the above bioassays, the potential risks, such as bacterial inactivation, bacterial membrane collapsed and thereafter intracellular leakage imposed by the outer stimulus (e.g., electricity, optical irradiation or oxidative stress exposure) are likely to occur. Unfortunately, there are so far few reports about the role of E. coli-inner content leakage on the detection signal.

Recently, as a unique chemiluminescence triggered by electrochemical methods, ECL assays with remarkable sensitivity, fine controllability, cheap instrumentation, and inherent miniaturization have attracted considerable attention (Richter et al., 2017; Qi et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2020). Several types of ECL immune/aptamer/peptide-based bioassays, have been developed for the E. coli detection, inaugurating an innovative field in bacterial analysis (Chen et al., 2017; Liu et al., 2019; Li et al. 2015, 2019; Hao et al., 2017). However, there still remain some challenges such as the costly biological receptors involved, relatively harsh conditions and coupling signal amplification strategies required, which impede their practical usage. Especially, whether in the typical anodic (e.g., luminal-H2O2)/cathodic (e.g., using S2O8 2− as coreactants) ECL system, the target of pathogenic bacteria is likely to suffer from the oxidative stress exposure, thus leading to bacterial inactivation and intracellular leakage. As far as we know, the application of intracellular leakage in the ECL sensing filed remains blank and some pioneering studies are urgently demanded. Inspired by this, we construct a novel solid-state ECL bioassay using the layer-by-layer (LBL) self-assembled multilayer film of N, S co-doped carbon dots (dCDs)–poly dimethyl diallyl ammonium chloride (PDDA) (labeled as (dCDs/PDDA) n ) as ECL luminophore with sodium peroxydisulfate (PS) as coreactants. As known, CDs, especially the heteroatom doped CDs are attractive due to their impressing biocompatibility, low costs and unique optical behaviors (Wu et al., 2019; Zuo et al., 2019). Through LBL assembly of dCDs and PDDA onto the surface of carbon nanospheres (CNs), a solid-state (dCDs/PDDA)n-grafted CNs ECL probe ((dCDs/PDDA)n@CNs) could be easily constructed. It successfully realized the immobilization of luminophore onto the localized solid substrate, featuring with enhanced stability, eliminating the dissolution loss, and reducing the consumption of expensive reactants (Wei et al., 2008). Likewise, the template directed molecularly imprinted electrospun nanofibers (ESNFs) were constructed onto the outmost (dCDs/PDDA) n layer as the recognition fragments (ESNFs-(dCDs/PDDA)n@CNs). With the specific recognition toward E. coli, a signal-off response would generally be observed due to the strong steric hindrance effect (Hao et al., 2017; Li et al., 2019). Unexpectedly, a greatly enhanced ECL response was achieved with the addition of E. coli in the cathodic dCDs-based ECL system using PS as coreactants (dCDs-PS). PS, a thermodynamically strong oxidant, possesses pathogen inactivation (Qi et al., 2018). Upon exposed to PS, the bacterial cell membrane would become damaged with the inner content released, small ions initially followed by large organic molecules. The released organic components could be revealed by monitoring UV absorption of bacterial suspension, an appropriate indication for membrane integrity of bacteria cells (Qian et al., 2011). Meanwhile, Potassium (K+) ions are the most dominant intracellular cations with high concentrations (0.1–10 mM) (Anand et al., 2007). Considering the reported effects of metal ions on ECL response, including quenching (via inner filter/electron transfer approach) or enhancing (via catalytic effect) ECL (Zhou et al., 2016), here, intracellular leakage K+-triggered ECL enhancement is first demonstrated, via boosting the involved 1O2-mediated ECL process. Benefiting from the unique ECL enhancement upon increasing the concentration of E. coli, an intracellular leakage-trigged signal-on ECL system has been created for sensing E. coli, via smartly integrating solid-state ensemble as ECL luminophore, ESNFs as the recognition fragments and the fantastic intracellular leakage-triggered signal amplification strategy (shown in Scheme 1 ). To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report on the intracellular leakage-triggered signal-on ECL bioassay for detecting E. coli. The resulting ECL bioassay is applicable for sensing E. coli in real water samples.
All chemicals and related instruments have been listed in Supporting Information.

Here, the dCDs and CNs were synthesized according to the literatures with some modifications, respectively (listed in Supporting Information). Through simple LBL assembly, the resulting ensemble of (dCDs/PDDA)3@CNs was constructed under the assistance of ultrasonication (listed in Supporting Information). ESNFs were prepared according to our previous method (Yang et al., 2017), using polystyrene as the polymer and E. coli as a template (Supporting Information). As a control, non-imprinted electrospun nanofibers (NINFs) were also fabricated without using template molecules.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the ECL sensor and ECL detection of E. coli
ce:section.ce:para: Typically, 2 mg of ESNFs powder was first dispersed into 1 mL of methanol for sonicating 12 h. Then the as-prepared suspension was mixed with 0.01 g mL−1 of (dCDs/PDDA)3@CNs dispersion in a certain volume ratio. After that, 20 μL of the mixed solution was dropped onto the surface of the cleaned glassy carbon electrode (GCE) and kept at room temperature till dry to obtain an ECL probe (ESNFs-(dCDs/PDDA)3@CNs/GCE). NINFs-(dCDs/PDDA)3@CNs/GCE electrode was also fabricated. All ECL measurements were recorded in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.0) solution containing 150 mM PS before and after the ESNFs-(dCDs/PDDA)3@CNs/GCE incubating into different concentration of E. coli for an certain time, respectively.

The as-prepared dCDs were characterized by UV–vis absorption and photoluminescence spectra (Fig. S1A), X-ray diffraction (XRD) (Fig. S1B), Fourier transforms infrared (FTIR) spectra (Fig. S1C), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Fig. S1D and Fig. S2). These results show that numerous nitrogen-, oxygen-, and sulphur-containing functional groups have been produced on the dCDs surface, demonstrating that S, N-co-doped CDs have been successfully synthesized. And the quantum yield of the as-prepared d-CDs was determined to be about 9.87% using quinine sulfate as a reference.

The narrow size distributions of the CNs and (dCDs/PDDA)3@CNs were demonstrated by scanning electrochemical microscopy (SEM) observation (Fig. 1 ). Compared with the original smooth CNs (~260 nm in average diameter, shown in Fig. 1A), after assembly, the surface of CNs became rough with grafting the ensemble of (dCDs/PDDA)3@CNs. The LBL assembly was also monitored through zeta potential measurements. The zeta potential for CNs, PDDA and dCDs dispersion alone is – 0.49, 0.32 and – 0.66 mV, respectively, indicating the building units with sufficient surface charge for the LBL assembly (Fig. 1C). Then with sequential layer of PDDA and dCDs coating onto the surface of CNs, the zeta potential alternated between positive and negative values, respectively. Obviously, the multilayer ensemble (dCDs/PDDA)3@CNs could be successfully fabricated by LBL assembly.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of ESNFs and the modified electrode
ce:section.ce:para: Electrospun polystyrene fibers with and without E. coli imprinted were studied by SEM and optical microscopy (Fig. 2 ). In the case of the polystyrene alone, uniform fibers with an average diameter of about 2 μm could be observed (Fig. 2A). While for the as-spun imprinted fibers, the beaded portion in fibers was observed, ascribed to the encapsulated bacteria (Fig. 2B). With a fluorescent dye-labeled E. coli imprinted, red fluorescence was observed clearly (Fig. 2C and Fig. S3). However, fluorescence disappeared with the imprinted E. coli removed (inset of Fig. 2C), implying the successful encapsulation of bacteria in fibers.

The as-spun imprinted fibers were further treated by the eluent solution to remove E. coli. The roles of the as-spun imprinted fibers are intensified by the UV–vis spectra of E. coli before and after reaching ESNFs-(dCDs/PDDA)3@CNs and NINFs-(dCDs/PDDA)3@CNs ensemble, respectively (Fig. 2D). The freshly prepared E. coli solution (108 cfu mL−1) showed a maximum absorbance at 260 nm (curve a). After the ESNFs-(dCDs/PDDA)n@CNs immersed in the E. coli solution for about 30 min, the absorbance showed an obvious decrease (curve c), indicating the uptake of E. coli by ESNFs. While the absorbance remained almost unchanged by immersing NINFs-(dCDs/PDDA)n@CNs into the solution (curve b). This further confirmed the specific recognition ability of ESNFs-(dCDs/PDDA)n@CNs toward E. coli.

The modification of ESNFs-(dCDs/PDDA)3@CNs ensemble onto GCE (ESNFs-(dCDs/PDDA)3@CNs/GCE) was further evidenced by the electrochemical impedance spectrum (EIS) measurements (Fig. 3 A). The EIS of the bare GCE is composed of a semicircle and a straight line, featuring a diffusion-limiting step of the [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- processes (curve a). With the modification of the E. coli-imprinted ESNFs-(dCDs/PDDA)3@CNs/GCE, the semicircle increased distinctively (curve b) due to the nonconductive properties of fibers. After removing the template of E. coli, the semicircles decreased (curve c), which could be attributed that removal of E. coli left a lot of cavities in ESNFs, thereby reducing the mass-transfer resistance of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− through the ESNFs film. After immersing ESNFs-(dCDs/PDDA)3@CNs into E. coli solution, the impedance increased significantly again (curve d), exhibiting specific recognition toward E. coli. The results confirm the successful modification of ESNFs-(dCDs/PDDA)n@CNs onto GCE surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ECL behaviors of the bioassay and possible mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 3B, by cycling the potential between 0.00 and −2.0 V (vs. Ag/AgCl), the ECL emission of bare GCE (curve a), CNs/GCE (curve b), and ESNFs/GCE (curve c) are negligible. However, upon assembling dCDs, an intense ECL emission of (dCDs/PDDA)3@CNs/GCE (curve d, around 6326) and ESNFs-(dCDs/PDDA)3@CNs/GCE (curve e, around 1286) could be observed, respectively, indicating a solid-state ECL probe was fabricated. With the rebinding of E. coli onto ESNFs-(dCDs/PDDA)3@CNs/GCE, unexpectedly, a greatly enhanced ECL response was observed (curve f, ~4309) in the present dCDs-PS ECL system, rather than a regular ECL quenching (Li et al., 2019). This remains unknown yet at present. Further studies should be carried out to exploit the possible mechanisms for the enhanced ECL signal.

As known, PS has a bactericidal effect, which can make cell content flow out by destroying the permeability barrier of cell membrane (Hao et al., 2019a; Qian et al., 2011). Upon the imprinted E. coli exposed to PS, under the sterilization effect of PS, the integrity of E. coli cell membrane would be destroyed with the intracellular components released. This case could be evidenced by the strong UV absorption at 260 nm (Fig. S4), ascribed to the released organic components (Anand et al., 2007). Whereas, the released organic components would obstruct the electron transfer due to the steric hindrance effect, resulting in ECL quenching instead of enhancement. Therefore, the role of the released organic components is excluded. The components of the intracellular leakage were further verified by XPS (Fig. 3C). The XPS survey scan of the intracellular leakage showed four characteristic peaks at about 285.5, 399.4 and 533.0 eV, corresponding to C1s, N1s, and O1s, further revealing the presence of organic intracellular components, agreeing well with the UV absorption results. The K2p peaks of the high-resolution XPS spectrum at 296.5 and 293.3 eV are shown in the inset of Fig. 3C, confirming the presence of K+ ions in intracellular leakage. Interestingly, if only K+ ions alone (e.g., 0.01 M) were added in the present dCDs-PS ECL system, the ECL intensity was significantly promoted raising from the initial ECL intensity ~ 1286 to ~ 2549 (inset of Fig. 3B). With increasing K+ ions concentration, the ECL intensity exhibited continuous increase. Obviously, in the present dCDs-PS ECL system, upon the imprinted E. coli exposed to PS, the greatly enhanced ECL intensity observed at ESNFs-(dCDs/PDDA)3@CNs/GCE is most likely to originate from the intracellular leakage K+, via promoting a certain ECL emission process. According to the previous reports (Chen et al., 2016), the reactions of the present dCDs-PS ECL system can proceed between dCDs∙− and SO4 ∙− (generated by the electro-reduction of PS) via typical equations (1)–(4), with dCDs as the light-emitting species.

In addition, accompanying the electrochemical reduction of PS, 1O2-mediated ECL process was worthy of attention though with small intensity (Reshetnyaka et al., 2003). Bƚazejowski et al. ever reported that the produced 1O2 molecule should be responsible for light-emitting during the reduction of PS. Among this process, SO4 ∙− electrochemically generated by PS could react with H2O to produce HO∙ radicals (via. Eq. (5)), and then the produced HO∙ radicals could disproportionate to give 1O2 in a highly exothermic and spontaneous process (Eq. (6)). Meanwhile, via a reductive protonation process, i.e. resorting two electron transfer of oxygen reduction (Eq. (7)), peroxy radicals (HOO∙) could be generated, and then HOO∙ radicals further combined with HO∙ or SO4 ∙− (Eq. (8)) to yield 1O2, behaving as the light-emitting specie. This could be evidenced by the weak ECL emission of bare GCE with PS alone (150 mM) in 0.1 M PBS solution, about 200 at − 1.97 V (inset of Fig. 3D, curve a). Notably, with the addition of K+ ions, the corresponding ECL intensity increased, around 416 (inset of Fig. 3D, curve b), suggesting that K+ ions contributed to the 1O2-mediated ECL process. In addition, the effects of dissolved oxygen on the ECL intensity were also investigated (shown in Fig. 3D). Upon expelling dissolved O2 by bubbling N2 into the solution, the ECL intensity of ESNFs-(dCDs/PDDA)3@CNs/GCE dramatically decreased (curve b), implying the significant participation of oxygen and electrochemically generated oxygen reactive species on the ECL process. With the addition of K+ into the deaerated PBS solution, the ECL intensity remained almost unchanged (curve c). Previous study ever demonstrated that K+ ions can significantly promote nitrogen reduction via facilitating the reductive protonation of N2 to *NNH (Hao et al., 2019b). In our case, K+ ions might contribute to the 1O2-mediated ECL process and K+-triggered ECL enhancement could be achieved by prompting the reduction of oxygen, a reductive protonation process from O2 to HOO∙ (Eq. (7)).

To further elucidate the possible reaction processes, the ECL responses on ESNFs-(dCDs/PDDA)3@CNs/GCE under different conditions were explored (Fig. 3D). With the introduction of the related radical scavengers into the air-saturated PBS solution, including isopropanol (HO∙ scavenger, curve d), furfuryl alcohol (1O2 scavenger, curve e) and methanol (SO4 ∙− scavenger, curve f), the corresponding ECL intensity exhibited about 17.3%, 30.8% and 38.6% decrease, respectively. In addition, compared with curve d, 53.8% of ECL intensity decrease was observed with the addition of thymol (scavengers of HO∙ and HOO∙) (Ashkan et al., 2014) into the air-saturated PBS solution (curve g), implying the contribution from HOO∙. It further confirmed that in the present dCDs-PS ECL system, besides the dominant ECL emission pathway using dCDs as luminophore, 1O2-mediated ECL process, including some reactive species (e.g. HO∙, SO4 ∙−, HOO∙ and 1O2) were indeed involved in the above ECL emission.

The intracellular leakage K+ mainly exerted its influence on 1O2-mediated ECL process via facilitating the reductive protonation of oxygen, thus triggering ECL enhancement. The possible mechanisms of the present cathodic dCDs-PS ECL system can be summarized in the following route:
(1) dCDs + e−→ dCDs∙−
(2) S2O8 2− + e−→ SO4 ∙− + SO4 2−
(3) dCDs∙− + SO4 ∙−→ dCDs* + SO4 2−
(4) dCDs*→ dCDs + hν
(5) SO4 ∙− + H2O → HO∙ + HSO4 −
(6) 2HO∙ → 1/2 1O2 + H2O
(7) O2 + H2O + 2e− → HOO∙ + HO−
(8)
H O O .
→
1
S
O 4
. -
H O .
O 2
(9)
1O2 → 3O2 + hν

To get high-performance ECL response, the related experimental parameters have been further optimized. Firstly, the influence of the pH of aqueous solutions on the ECL response of ESNFs-(dCDs/PDDA)3@CNs/GCE was investigated (Fig. 4 A). With the pH value ranged from 6.0 to 8.0, the ECL signal increased up to pH 7.0 and then dropped. At a lower pH value, protons were easily reduced at the negative potential, impeding the reduction of oxygen. Considering the bioactivity of E. coli, the pH value was optimized to be 7.0. Since the ECL efficiency depends on the formation rate of excited species and the diffusion rate of the coreactants, too low or high scan rates will affect the ECL intensity. The optimal scan rate was 300 mV s−1 (Fig. 4B). Fig. 4C showed the effect of PS concentration on the ECL response of ESNFs-(dCDs/PDDA)3@CNs/GCE. When the concentration of PS was 150 mM, the maximum ECL intensity was reached. With further increasing the PS concentration, the ECL intensity would decay due to the dimerization reaction of SO4 ∙−.

As ECL luminophore, the amount of dCDs in ESNFs-(dCDs/PDDA)3@CNs ensemble remarkably affects the ECL intensity. The total volume of the dispersion casting onto GCE and the volume ratio of dispersion of (dCDs/PDDA)3@CNs to ESNFs were studied for the preparation of ESNFs-(dCDs/PDDA)3@CNs/GCE, respectively. The ECL emission of ESNFs-(dCDs/PDDA)3@CNs/GCE gradually increased till the total volume of the dispersion up to 20 μL and then dropped. Thus, the optimal volume of the dispersion casting onto GCE was 20 μL (shown in Fig. 4D). While fixing 20 μL of the total volume and varying the volume ratio of dispersion of (dCDs/PDDA)3@CNs to ESNFs, the ECL response initially increased and then decreased with the volume ratio up to 2:1 (Fig. 4E). Fig. 4F showed the effect of the reaction time on the ECL intensity by immersing the ESNFs-(dCDs/PDDA)3@CNs/GCE into E. coli solution. The ECL signal steadily increased till immersion time up to 10 min and then declined. Therefore, the optimal immersion time was 10 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance for detection of E. coli
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimized experimental conditions, as shown in Fig. 5 A, the ECL intensity of ESNFs-(dCDs/PDDA)3@CNs/GCE increased gradually with increasing the concentration of E. coli. As the concentration of E. coli increase, more E. coli was bound to sensing assay of ESNFs-(dCDs/PDDA)3@CNs/GCE. Under the bactericidal effect of PS, the permeability barrier of E. coli cell membrane was broken, thereby causing the cell contents flowing out. The intracellular leakage K+ ions triggered ECL enhancement via boosting the coupled 1O2-mediated ECL process. There was a linear response between the ECL intensity and the logarithmic value of the concentration of E. coli in a range from 5 to 107 cfu mL−1. The linearization equations were: ECL intensity = 528.93 + 1457.74 lgC/cfu mL−1 (R = 0.993, Fig. 5B) with the detection limit determined as 1.0 cfu mL−1 (Signal/Noise = 3). The obtained detection limit is dramatically lower than 2.3 × 102 cfu mL−1 of E. coli with a ECL bioassay using antimicrobial peptides as capture probes (Li et al., 2015) and 143 cfu mL−1 of E. coli with a Ru(bpy)3 2+/ZnO nanorod arrays-based ECL immunosensor (Liu et al., 2019); also lower than that obtained by ECL immunosensor on smart phone (5 cfu mL−1) (Li et al., 2019). Obviously, compared with the previous reports for E. coli detection (shown in Table S1), the proposed intracellular leakage-trigged signal-on ECL assay, without using any expensive and fragile biological receptor, demonstrates a lower detection limit and a wider linear detection range for E. coli detection. And the whole ECL measurement could be finished in 11min (including 10 min of the immersion time in E. coli).

The specificity of the present bioassay toward E. coli was assessed by evaluating samples containing some typical amino acids and bacterial with similarity of E. coli in species. Typical amino acids, e.g. Glycine (Gly), Threonine (Thre), Aspartic (Asp), Serine (Ser) and Histidine (His) were chosen as the interfering substances. It can be seen that the ECL response toward 100 μM of each amino acid compound showed negligible change with that of the blank (Fig. 5C). It was further corroborated by evaluating solution containing bacterial with similarity of E. coli in species, such as Gram-negative Salmonella (Sal), Pseudomonas aeruginosa (P. aer) and Gram-positive Staphylococcus aureus (S. aur) in a concentration of 104 cfu mL−1 each, exhibiting the similar ECL intensity to the blank. Even in a two-component system with the coexistence of E. coli (103 cfu mL−1), the ECL intensity deviations are all less than 5%. This adequately revealed that the as-prepared ECL bioassay was highly specific for E. coli. When applied in real samples, the achieved signal could be linked only to E. coli detection.

To evaluate the stability and reproducibility of the present ECL system in air-saturated PBS solution containing 150 mM PS, the replicated cyclic voltammetric sweeps (n = 10) are performed (Fig. 5D). Strong and highly repeatable ECL signals were obtained, further demonstrating its feasibility for ECL sensing E. coli. And the ECL intensity could retain about 88.8% of its initial response after repeating usage up to 5 cycles (shown in Fig. S5), indicating a good recycle performance.

To further explore the practicability of the bioassay for the E. coli detection, the ECL signal of the assay was observed by spiking known concentrations of E. coli into tap water and river samples collected from the East Lake (Wuhan, China). The obtained recoveries were observed to be ranged from 94.7% to 107.8% (Table S2). There was no drastic interference when evaluating real sample in a complex matrix, promising for the E. coli detection in real samples at the point of care (PoC) tests.

In summary, a novel intracellular leakage-trigged signal-on solid-state ECL bioassay has been successfully fabricated for the detection of E. coli by smartly integrating the (dCDs/PDDA)n@CNs ensemble as ECL luminophore, ESNFs as the recognition fragments and the intracellular leakage-trigged signal amplification strategy. The possible mechanism of intracellular leakage K+-trigged ECL enhancement was first proposed via contributing to a 1O2-mediated ECL process. Such an intracellular leakage-trigged signal-on ECL bioassay not only efficiently eliminates the dissolution loss of luminophore, avoids the use of costly biological receptors, but also enriches the ECL signal amplification strategy. The resulting bioassay exhibits fine applicability for sensing E. coli in real samples, holding promise for PoC tests. The further work is undergoing to explore the present protocol for detection of pathogenic bacteria and their antibiotic resistance.

Yuxin Chen: Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Shumin Jiang: Investigation, Software, Formal analysis. Yachen Hu: Validation, Investigation. Jingming Gong: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 22076057, 21777052), the Project for Application Foundation Frontier for Wuhan (Grant 2019020701011486), and The Program of Introducing Talents of Discipline to Universities of China (111 program, B17019).
